title: More Decadence
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-18-2025 - AM - More Decadence - Hosea 7 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Israel Needed to Turn — But Turned Everywhere Except to God
subpoints: This is the 18th sermon in a series of 15 — now chapter 7 of Hosea
Isaiah's 50+ years of prophecy could be read in 30-40 minutes — patient faithfulness
By chapter 7, Israel realizes they need to turn to someone — but they turn to Egypt and Assyria, not to the Lord
Though the Lord pursued them (vv. 15-16), they didn't turn to him
The simplest explanation: they knew turning to God meant turning from the sin they loved

outline.title: Four Similes: Israel's Condition Before God
subpoints: God uses four similes (illustrations) to show Israel's condition
1. Israel is like a burning hot oven (vv. 3-7) — a demonstration of their passion for evil
2. Israel is like a half-baked cake (vv. 8-10) — spiritually useless and self-deceived
3. Israel is like a silly dove (vv. 11-12) — foolishly seeking help from the wrong sources
4. Israel is like a treacherous bow (vv. 13-16) — telling lies about God by their behavior

outline.title: Like a Burning Hot Oven (vv. 3-7): Cultivated Passion for Evil
subpoints: The oven is being prepared not to bake nourishing food but to inflame sin even more abundantly
The heart is set ablaze (v. 6) by sexual sin, drunkenness, and fellowship with the enemies of the Lord
The priests are responsible for leading Israel away from God — doubly accountable (Matthew 18:6)
Sin begets sin: like Scotch broom introduced to the Pacific Northwest — small and 'cute,' then invasive and destructive
We cannot tame sin or keep it to one area of our lives
John Owen: "Be killing sin or sin be killing you"
The result: five kings destroyed in 20 years — sin destroys those around it
Proverbs 9: sin invites you to a banquet, but it is a banquet in the grave
The ultimate sin in this passage: "None among them calls upon me" (v. 7) — the ultimate sin is unrepentance

outline.title: Like a Half-Baked Cake (vv. 8-10): Useless and Unaware
subpoints: An unturned cake is burned on one side and raw on the other — good for nothing
Foreigners (those who do not worship the true God) have devoured Israel's strength — but they don't even know it
Secular counseling dominates evangelical ministry — the foreigners are controlling how we think, and we don't even realize it
"Gray hairs are here and there on him, yet he does not know it" — Israel is decaying and unaware
The pie competition illustration: so proud of an inedible, raw, salty pie
Israel would rather not be helped by the Lord than turn to him — the ultimate display of pride
The ultimate sin again: unbelief and lack of repentance

outline.title: Like a Silly Dove (vv. 11-12): Seeking Help in All the Wrong Places
subpoints: Israel made alliances with both Egypt and Assyria — hedging between two opposing superpowers
This was foolish: if either side found out, it would mean the end of Israel
They are trying to flee from God's judgment by going to Egypt and Assyria rather than running to the Lord
"Wherever they go, I will spread my net on them" (v. 12) — there is no fleeing the judgment of God in rebellion

outline.title: Like a Treacherous Bow (vv. 13-16): Lies Told About God
subpoints: A treacherous bow does not shoot straight — it is useless
Israel bore the name of God as their covenant people, and so every action told a story about God
"They have spoken lies against me" (v. 13) — their lives were telling lies about who God is
Rocky's USDA stamp illustration: baptism is God's seal on us — we do everything in God's name
"How many lies are you telling about your Savior by the way you think, speak, and behave?"
An unrepentant Christian is a useless Christian
Egypt is the picture of exile and slavery — that's where Israel ends when they refuse to repent

outline.title: Conclusion: What Is Repentance and Who Must Repent?
subpoints: Repentance is a turning from sin and a turning to God
Repentance includes genuine sorrow for sin (Matthew 11; 2 Corinthians 7:10-11)
Repentance includes genuine hatred for sin and forsaking of it (Ezekiel 18; Joel 2)
Repentance results in a firm resolve to walk in holiness — turning to God
When listed with faith, repentance is always listed first: "Repent and believe in the gospel"
No sin is too small to repent of; no sin is too large (David, Elijah's apostasy, Peter's denial, the crucifixion itself)
Acts 17:30: "God now commands all men everywhere to repent"
God calls you to repent — not your neighbor, not your spouse — you

summary: Continuing the Hosea series (now in its 18th installment, far beyond the original plan of 15), this sermon takes up Hosea 7:1-16. Pastor Tito Lyro frames the passage by noting that by chapter 7, Israel recognized the need to turn somewhere — but turned to Egypt and Assyria rather than to God, because they knew that turning to God meant turning from the sin they loved. God uses four extended similes to describe Israel's condition: a burning hot oven (passion for evil, cultivated rather than restrained); a half-baked cake (spiritually useless, controlled by worldly ideas without recognizing it); a silly dove (frantically seeking help from wrong sources rather than from God); and a treacherous bow (useless because it tells lies about God through the behavior of his covenant people). Across all four images, the central sin is the same: lack of repentance, refusal to call upon God. Notable illustrations include the Scotch broom invasive species (sin cannot be contained to one area), the pie competition (pride in something that is actually inedible), and the Rocky USDA stamp (baptism as God's seal meaning we do everything in his name). The sermon closes with a careful definition of repentance: genuine sorrow for sin, genuine hatred for and forsaking of sin, and a firm resolve to walk in holiness. Repentance and faith are always paired — with repentance listed first. No sin is too small to repent of, and no sin is too large. The sermon ends with a universal call to repent, citing Acts 17:30.
transcript:
## Introduction

For those able, would you remain standing for a moment longer as we read God's Word? We're reading from the book of Hosea chapter 7. We're going to read verses 1 through 16 of Hosea chapter 7. God's people, this is God's Word.

"When I would have healed Israel, then the iniquity of Ephraim was uncovered, and the wickedness of Samaria. For they have committed fraud; a thief comes in; a band of robbers raids outside. They do not consider in their hearts that I remember all their wickedness. Now their own deeds have surrounded them; they are before my face. They make a king glad with their wickedness, and princes with their lies. They are all adulterous, like an oven heated by a baker — he ceases stirring the fire after kneading the dough, until it is leavened. In the day of our king princes have made him sick, inflamed with wine. He stretched out his hand with scoffers. They prepared their heart like an oven, while they lie in wait; their baker sleeps all night; in the morning it burns like a flaming fire. They are all hot like an oven, and have devoured their judges; all their kings have fallen. None among them calls upon me. Ephraim has mixed himself among the peoples; Ephraim is a cake unturned. Aliens have devoured his strength, but he does not know it; yes, gray hairs are here and there on him, yet he does not know it. And the pride of Israel testifies to his face, but they do not return to the Lord their God, nor seek him for all this. Ephraim also is like a silly dove, without sense — they call to Egypt, they go to Assyria. Wherever they go, I will spread my net on them; I will bring them down like birds of the air; I will chastise them according to what their congregation has heard. Woe to them, for they have fled from me! Destruction to them, because they have transgressed against me! Though I redeemed them, yet they have spoken lies against me. They do not cry out to me with their heart when they wail upon their beds. They assemble together for grain and new wine; they rebel against me. Though I disciplined and strengthened their arms, yet they devise evil against me. They return, but not to the Most High; they are like a treacherous bow. Their princes shall fall by the sword for the cursings of their tongue. This shall be their derision in the land of Egypt."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, sometimes we read from the prophets and it can be confusing and difficult to understand. So we pray that you open our eyes to understand what you have for us this morning. I pray that you would use my words for the good of your people. I pray that your Spirit would preach a better sermon than I can this morning. In asking Jesus name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: Israel Needed to Turn

You know, because we can read the book of Hosea aloud in about 30 to 40 minutes, we often forget that this prophecy was delivered in Israel in a period of at least 50 years. What we can read in 30 to 40 minutes was actually delivered over 50 plus years in the 8th century before Christ. Hosea faithfully brought God's word to the people of God for decades, even though it was not the word that the people of God wanted. And he persevered in doing that.

This is our 18th sermon out of a series of 15 sermons in the book of Hosea. This was supposed to be the 15th sermon on the original plan. But we're moving — we have a whole chapter before us today. We're going to be in Hosea chapter 7, whether we finish it today or not, and moving on the Lord willing to chapter 8 next time.

By the time we get to chapter 7 in the narrative of Hosea, Israel is aware that they need to turn to someone. Things are not the way that they should be. Things need to change. It's not okay to continue the way they are. They need help. They even realize that what they are doing is not working. Their kings are falling — we see that in verses 3 through 7 — and foreigners who don't worship Yahweh have overtaken the land, in verses 8 through 10. Yet though realizing they needed to turn to someone for help, they did not turn to the Lord — we see that in verse 16 and throughout the passage. They instead turned to Egypt and Assyria, as we see in verse 10.

Now why did they not turn to the Lord? The simplest explanation is that they knew that turning to the Lord also meant turning from the sin that they loved so much. They didn't want to leave their sin. They had enough sense to know that they couldn't turn to the Lord without turning away from their sin. And even though the Lord pursued them — we see that in verses 15 and 16 — they didn't turn to him.

This is a passage in which the Lord is the first-person speaker. All the "I's" in it refer to God himself. He's speaking and he uses four similes. A simile is when you say that something is like something else. He uses four similes to show us the condition of Israel.

- Israel is like a burning hot oven (verses 3-7)
- Israel is like a half-baked cake (verses 8-10)
- Israel is like a silly dove (verses 11-12)
- Israel is like a treacherous bow (verses 13-16)

And that's the outline we're going to use for this morning's sermon.

## Like a Burning Hot Oven (vv. 3-7): Passion for Evil

God says that Israel is like a hot burning oven in verses 3-7. This is a demonstration of their passion for evil. As you read these verses, the "I's" are about God; the "days" and "thems" and "theirs" are likely about the prophet and the priests. Since chapter 4, verse 4, God has been indicting the priests of Israel for leading Israel away from the true God and unto the worship of false gods. In Hosea 4:4, the text of the ESV says: "Yet let no one contend, and let no one accuse, for with you is my contention, O priest."

The priests had such power over the people that they controlled even the kings and the princes. Every person is accountable for his or her own sins — but it is an even more heinous thing to lead other people to sin against God. In Ezekiel 18:20 the Lord tells us that every person is accountable for their own sins. But in Matthew 18:6, our Lord himself says: "Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were drowned in the depths of the sea."

Parents, and especially fathers: your children will be accountable for their own sins. But may God be merciful to you if you are leading them astray from the living God — you will be doubly accountable for that.

Not only were the priests leading the people away, the rulers rejoiced in the wickedness of the priests and the people — we see that in verse 3: "They make a king glad with their wickedness and princes with their lies."

What does it mean for Israel to be a hot oven? The baker prepares the oven to accomplish one thing: bake food for nourishment. He loads it with the wood, he stokes it so that it heats evenly, he lets it smolder so the temperature rises. The priests were doing the same thing with Israel — but instead of preparing them to produce what nourishes, the priests were preparing them to sin even more abundantly.

In verse 6, the Lord tells us that the oven being heated up is the heart. Their hearts are inflamed with sin — and this is sin that is cultivated, not sin that catches you by surprise. The heart is set ablaze by sexual sin, by drunkenness, by fellowship with the scoffers and the enemies of the Lord (see verses 4 and 5).

Brothers and sisters, sin begets sin. Sin, even the smallest sin, cultivates the heart to dominate the heart. If you drive anywhere here, you see Scotch broom. There's actually even a state fund to remove Scotch broom — this ubiquitous bush with the yellow flowers that seems so cute, unless you have pollen allergies. Some well-intentioned person in the mid-1800s decided it would be a good idea to bring Scotch broom from Central Europe to plant here to keep the land from eroding. They thought they could keep it just in the areas where they planted it. They had all these plans. Well, we all know that didn't work. Soon Scotch broom escaped the designated areas, and today it displaces native plants and animals, increases wildfire risk, and alters soil chemistry.

Sin is just like that, brothers and sisters. We think we can control it. We think we can keep it to just one area of our lives. We think that as long as 98% of our heart belongs to the Lord, we can keep this 2% of darkness in one corner. But we cannot tame sin, and we cannot keep it under control in just one area of our lives. The Apostle Paul uses the example of leaven: a little leaven leavens the whole lump. And that is why our Lord himself tells us that we have to deal with sin radically. If your hand causes you to sin, cut it off. If your eye causes you to sin, pluck it out. Of course our Lord is not encouraging us to literally do that — but he's telling us to be radical about dealing with sin.

John Owen is famously credited with saying: "Be killing sin or sin will be killing you." Those are the only two options. Israel thought they could compartmentalize sin. It did not work for them — that's why we have the book of Hosea. It does not work for you either.

The heat of their hearts inflamed with sin is destroying themselves — starting with the kings. Look at verse 7: "They are all hot like an oven, and have devoured their judges; all their kings have fallen." In the space of 20 years in the period Hosea is writing about, 5 kings are destroyed because of the sin of Israel. Shallum assassinated Zechariah, Menahem assassinated Shallum after only one month on the throne, then Pekah assassinated Pekahiah, Hoshea then assassinated Pekah, and then Hoshea was put in prison by the Assyrians after having his eyes burned. That's the result of cultivated sin.

Sin destroys, no matter how attractive it seems. In Proverbs chapter 9, the Holy Spirit gives us this great imagery of the attractiveness of sin, personifying sin as the foolish woman. In verses 13-16: "A foolish woman is clamorous...she sits at the door of her house on a seat by the highest places of the city, to call to those who pass by...who said, 'Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant.'" Sin is there saying, "Come on in! We have a great meal for you here." And then the Holy Spirit concludes: "But he does not know that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of hell." Brothers and sisters, sin invites you to a banquet. But it is a banquet in the grave.

As a side note: Hosea delivered what is today chapter 7 before any of these things happened to the kings. All this happened in his future, which tells us that Hosea passes the test of a biblical prophet — his prophecy actually happened.

And the ultimate sin in this passage, at the very end of verse 7, is this: "None among them calls upon me." The ultimate sin is lack of repentance. Ultimately, the only sin for which a person goes to hell and bears the wrath of God is unbelief — demonstrated in the lack of repentance. Nobody goes to hell for any other reason. Because every sin is forgiven if you come to the Lord Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.

## Like a Half-Baked Cake (vv. 8-10): Useless and Unaware

We have several good bakers in the church, and my wife is getting to be the best sourdough baker in all the world. We always have sourdough at our house — bread, pizza, English muffins, hamburger buns, anything else sourdough. But her sourdough is edible. That's not Israel. Israel is an unturned cake. An unturned cake is one that is not fit for eating — one side is burned to a crisp and the other side is raw. Good for nothing.

Their passion for evil makes them good for nothing. Their half-bakedness has allowed them to be manipulated by foreigners, and they don't even know it. Verse 9: "Aliens have devoured his strength, but he does not know it."

Now before you get the idea that the Bible is against immigrants — that's not what this passage says. A "foreigner" here is one who does not worship the true God of the Bible. So you can be a red-white-and-blue American and be a foreigner in God's terms if you do not worship the true God of the Bible. What is God saying here? That in their half-bakedness, those who do not worship God are controlling them and they don't even notice it. They're not thinking like those who belong to God. They're letting worldly ideas dominate the way they think and the way they behave.

Is that true of the church today? I think in the area of counseling, even evangelicalism is dominated by secular ideas that are diametrically opposed to what the Bible teaches. We're letting the foreigners — those who don't believe in God — dictate how we think about even how to minister to one another. And we don't even realize that is the case.

Their half-bakedness is also affecting their self-assessment in verse 9: "Aliens have devoured his strength, but he does not know it. Yes, gray hairs are here and there on him, yet he does not know it." Israel is like the guy who is getting older and weaker but still thinks he can do what he did in high school. Israel is unaware that time is running out on them. The gray hair is a sign of decay and approaching end.

Verse 10: "And the pride of Israel testifies to his face." It's like bringing an inedible, raw, salty pie to a pie competition and being incredibly proud of it — only to watch it be thrown up immediately by the judges. That's Israel. They're looking at themselves and thinking, "How great we are!" And yet time is running out.

The ultimate display of this pride is that they are not turning to the Lord. Israel would rather not be helped by the Lord than turn to him. And that testifies against them. We see again: the ultimate sin is unbelief and lack of repentance.

## Like a Silly Dove (vv. 11-12): Seeking Help in All the Wrong Places

Being the excellent preacher that he is, the Lord uses a third illustration. Verses 11-12: "Ephraim also is like a silly dove, without sense — they call to Egypt, they go to Assyria. Wherever they go, I will spread my net on them."

Israel thought that it would be a good idea to make alliances with two opposing superpowers — Egypt and Assyria. They thought they could hedge their situation by deceiving the most powerful kingdoms around them. They sent emissaries to Egypt, then turned around and sent to Assyria as well. Knowing that if either side found out what they're doing, it would be the end of Israel. That was a foolish thing to do.

Israel thinks it can find relief from the judgment that is coming from the Lord by going to Egypt and Assyria. They are trying to run away from the Lord instead of running to the Lord. But there is no place where the rebellious can flee the judgment of God — verse 12: "Wherever they go, I will spread my net on them."

## Like a Treacherous Bow (vv. 13-16): Lies Told About God

In case Israel still thinks God's message remains unclear, the Lord uses a fourth simile. Verses 13-16: Israel is like a treacherous bow — a bow that does not shoot straight and therefore is useless.

Does anyone here bow hunt in the fall? The idea is that if your bow is not calibrated, it doesn't matter how good of a shooter you are — it's not going to shoot where you want. I was at the gym last week, and on one of the TVs was a cartoon of Sylvester the Cat trying to shoot an arrow at Tweety Bird. Tweety comes and bends the arrow so that when Sylvester shoots, it curves around like a boomerang and hits him in the back. How useful is that? A bow that will not shoot the arrow where you want it to go. That's Israel — because of its rebellion against God.

Verse 13: "Well to them, for they have fled from me! Destruction to them, because they have transgressed against me! Though I redeemed them, yet they have spoken lies against me." Israel bore the name of God as their covenant people. Every time they did anything, they did it as the people of God. And every action they took was telling lies about who God is.

Brothers and sisters, you and I bear the name of Christ. You have been baptized — you are baptized into the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. You have that stamp. Remember Rocky One? Where he trains in the meat locker with the frozen carcasses, and on each side of beef there's a purple USDA stamp saying, "Yes, this is meat." The stamp doesn't cause it to be meat, but it says it's meat. We've been baptized with the seal that God's name stands upon us. And everything we do, we do in God's name. How many lies are you telling about your Savior by the way you think, by the way you speak, and by the way that you behave? Are you a useless bow, like Israel was?

And on top of that, they did not turn to the Lord. Verse 14: "They do not cry out to me with their heart when they wail upon their beds." An unrepentant Christian is a useless Christian. Israel's rebellion resulted in destruction — verse 16: Egypt is the picture of exile and slavery, and that's where Israel ends.

## Conclusion: What Is Repentance?

As we look at all these illustrations, you see that the clearest issue with Israel is lack of repentance. With each illustration the Lord uses, the main point is that Israel did not turn to the Lord. Repentance is so important.

So what is repentance? Repentance is a turning from sin and a turning to God.

Repentance includes genuine sorrow for sin. The Lord Jesus speaking in Matthew 11 says: "If the mighty works which were done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes." A genuine sorrow for sin — godly sorrow produces repentance (2 Corinthians 7:10-11).

Repentance includes genuine hatred for sin and forsaking of sin. Paul says: "What diligence this producing in you, what clearing of yourselves, what indignation, what fear...what vehement desire, what zeal, what vindication!" In Ezekiel 18 the Lord says: "Cast away from you all the transgressions which you've committed." In the new covenant in Ezekiel 36, it says: "You will loathe yourselves in your own sight for your iniquities and your abominations." In Joel chapter 2: "Turn to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning."

Brothers, do you hate your sin? Or do you think that's nothing, that you can keep it to yourself? Your sin is going to be the end of yourself. Turn to the Lord, forsake it, hate your sin, and repent unto God.

Repentance results in a firm resolve to walk in holiness — a turning to God. The Apostle Paul tells the Ephesian elders that his entire ministry was proclaiming repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ. When listed with faith or conversion, repentance is always listed first. The Lord Jesus says: "Repent and believe in the gospel." Paul says: "Repent, turn to God, and do works worthy of repentance."

Repentance must be preached. If we ever stop preaching repentance, this church fails. Without repentance, there is no salvation. Christ says that part of the Great Commission is the preaching of repentance. In Luke 24, the Lord says that the apostles are to go out and that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name to all nations.

What should we repent of? All sins. Our confession says, "As there is no sin so small but it deserves damnation, so there is no sin so great that it can bring damnation upon those who truly repent." So no sin is too small — James 2:10: "For whoever shall keep the whole law and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all." But friends, no sin is too big either. Think of David's sin — destruction of a nation — yet God forgave him. Think of Elijah's apostasy, and yet God called him to come and repent. Think of Peter's denial of his Savior, and God forgave him. And then the greatest sin of all: the crucifixion of the Lord Jesus Christ — that not only that generation was guilty of, but you and I are guilty of. And yet he forgave them, and he's forgiven us.

Acts 17:30: "Truly, these times of ignorance God overlooked, but now commands all men everywhere to repent." If you are human, that command is to you. All men everywhere. And yet our Lord says: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9).

Brothers and sisters, he who knew no sin became guilty of sin — your sin — so that you may be made the righteousness of God. We have a Savior who is willing to take the guilt of our sins upon him, that we might be forgiven.

Let's pray together. Father, thank you that you are a forgiving God. Help us to live a life of repentance before you. If there are those among us who have never repented of their sins, grant to them to repent today, that they might know you are forgiven. In asking Jesus' name. Amen.
